title: Extending Flexibility of Image Coding Enhancement Framework for IoTs

reviews.summary: This paper proposes Easz, an edge-to-server image coding enhancement framework designed to reduce sender-side computation while enabling flexible control over the effective compression level. The sender applies an erase-and-squeeze operation driven by a “conditional uniform-based” sampler that removes a subset of pixels/patches and packs the remaining ones; on the receiver, a lightweight transformer with a two-stage patchifying strategy reconstructs the erased content. The method is claimed to be compatible with standard codecs (e.g., JPEG, BPG) and learned codecs, to offer fine-grained compression flexibility via mask parameters, and to yield improved perceptual quality/latency trade-offs on a Jetson-TX2-to-server testbed compared with deep learned codecs and super-resolution baselines.
strengths: The erase-and-squeeze pipeline that decouples sender-side sampling from receiver-side reconstruction is a pragmatic systems-oriented angle on image coding for resource-constrained edges.

The row-wise random sampler with intra-/inter-row spacing constraints is a simple, codec-agnostic strategy to obtain non-uniform sampling while keeping a rectangular organization amenable to standard codecs.
The two-stage patchifying with a small transformer to localize attention and enable pixel-level masked-image-modeling-like reconstruction is a well-motivated efficiency compromise.
Real-device measurements on NVIDIA Jetson TX2 quantify encode latency, power, and memory footprint, which are often missing in learned-compression papers.
The framework is tested as an enhancement for multiple codecs (JPEG, BPG, MBT, Cheng-Anchor) and evaluated on two datasets (Kodak, CLIC) with perceptual metrics and a latency breakdown.
The system-level motivation is clear: edge devices cannot run heavy encoders; using classical codecs with a downstream learned reconstructor is appealing in practice.
The flexibility argument (controlling erase level via T and patch size p) is intuitive and repeatedly emphasized.
The work addresses a real deployment gap (edge constraints and codec inflexibility) and suggests a path to adaptively trade bits for server compute without switching between multiple codec models.
If validated and generalized, this approach could inform modular designs that complement existing codecs rather than replace them.
weaknesses: The “conditional uniform-based” sampler is heuristic and not learned/content-adaptive; it may underperform compared to content-aware sampling/ROI coding while being less principled than MIM mask designs used in MAE/inpainting literature.

The claim that a single small per-subpatch mask (e.g., 128 bytes) is reused “for all sub-patches” seems inconsistent with the described row-wise constrained sampling that references previous-row samples; it is unclear how a fixed mask simultaneously satisfies those constraints and supports per-image flexibility.

Complexity analysis contains inconsistencies and some formulae are imprecise; the final O(h·w·n²) result and intermediate steps are not rigorously derived and omit constants relevant to transformer attention.
Pretraining solely on CIFAR-10 for high-resolution reconstruction is unusual; domain gap implications are not quantified, and details on fine-tuning (epochs, data split, early stopping) are sparse.

No direct rate–distortion evaluation with standard full-reference metrics on like-for-like content where applicable (e.g., for the learned codecs) and no evaluation including the mask overhead in BPP.

Comparisons to deep codecs on Jetson TX2 may not be apples-to-apples (implementation maturity, hardware acceleration paths, quantization, lower-complexity presets); broader baselines like lightweight CNN-based inpainting/super-resolution under matched compute would strengthen conclusions.
The “classification robustness” claim is mentioned but results are not reported.

The sampler description is ambiguous: row-wise sampling with inter-row spacing while also proposing a single reusable mask per subpatch contradicts earlier constraints that depend on previously sampled columns.
The definition of several reported metrics (e.g., “file-saving ratio”) and some plotting choices are underspecified; error bars or variance analysis are missing.
The two-stage patchify diagrams help intuition, but key architecture hyperparameters (embed dims, heads, exact tokenization) are insufficiently specified for reproducibility.
The paper does not situate itself against masked autoencoders (MAE) and modern inpainting/partial-convolution approaches that are highly relevant to the reconstruction task from sparse observations.
Adaptive/foveated compression and content-weighted learned compression (e.g., ROI coding, spatially varying bit allocation, non-uniform sampling for reconstruction) are under-cited and not compared.
Joint source–channel coding and learned “post-processing” restorers for legacy codecs (e.g., deblocking/denoising networks) provide alternative pipelines that merit discussion.
reviews.questions:
1. How exactly is the mask transmitted and accounted for in BPP? If a single 32×32 mask is reused for all sub-patches, how does this reconcile with the row-wise sampler’s inter-row constraints that appear to depend on row index i and past selections?
2. What are the transformer’s full architectural details (embedding dimension, number of heads, MLP ratio, positional encoding, tokenization channel handling), and how sensitive are results to these choices?
3. Can you report classification accuracy on reconstructed images vs baselines at matched BPP (with mask overhead), and provide examples where reconstruction harms analytics due to hallucination?
4. How are δ and Δ chosen and scaled with image size and patch size? Please include an ablation/sensitivity study and a learned alternative (even if trained on the server) to understand the benefit of the heuristic constraints.
5. Do end-to-end results change when including mask bits in the payload, especially for varying erase ratios and patch sizes?
6. Could you compare against compact CNN-based reconstruction (e.g., U-Net-lite) at the same parameter count and latency to justify the transformer choice?
7. For fairness, can you evaluate MBT/Cheng-Anchor with speed-oriented settings (e.g., smaller models or quantization) on TX2, and report both quality and latency at matched BPP?
8. In the super-resolution comparison, please specify exact sampling ratios, degradations, and whether all methods reconstruct from identical inputs. Are the SR baselines allowed to see the same (squeezed) image and mask?
reviews.assessment: This paper targets an important and pragmatic problem: enabling flexible, low-overhead image coding on resource-constrained edges by offloading heavy computation to the server while remaining compatible with standard codecs. The system design is sensible and the real-device measurements are a welcome contribution. The proposed sampler and lightweight reconstruction are straightforward and could be practically useful. However, the technical novelty is moderate, with close connections to established masked/inpainting reconstruction and content-adaptive sampling lines of work. More concerning are clarity inconsistencies (particularly around mask reuse versus row-wise constraints), incomplete accounting for mask overhead in rate–distortion, and limited fairness in comparisons to learned codecs on constrained hardware. The experimental section, while broad, lacks several critical controls and ablations that would convincingly substantiate the central claims. With stronger methodological rigor (clear mask accounting, fair baselines, additional architectural ablations, and inclusion of relevant related work such as MAE/inpainting and adaptive compression), this line of work could be impactful. As it stands, I view the submission as promising but not yet at the level of technical completeness and clarity expected for ICLR acceptance.
